description: `npm install` followed by `npm run build` exits 0 and produces `dist/index.js` plus every module under `src/`. `node dist/index.js` with a missing config file logs a fatal error and exits non-zero; with a valid `relay-config.yaml` (or the documented env-var fallbacks) the process starts the pino logger, logs "Starting CauseFlow Relay...", loads the config, and begins attempting to connect to `controlPlane.url` (failing fast on bad config but retrying on bad URL until the WS reaches the control plane).
steps: Read the Acceptance Check AC-001 description in project_specs.xml to understand the contract
Locate the relevant source file(s) under src/ (and configuration files at the repo root) referenced by AC-001
Execute the documented verification path (commands, env, and inputs from the AC) and observe the expected outcome
Confirm the output / state matches what AC-001 asserts; record the result for the harness journal

description: `docker build . --platform linux/amd64` (and on a buildx host, `--platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64`) succeeds and produces an image that, when run with a valid config, starts as UID 10001, logs the same "Starting CauseFlow Relay..." line, and connects to the configured control plane. `docker run --rm <image> node -e "require('fs').readFileSync('/etc/passwd','utf8')"` shows the image runs as `relay` (UID 10001) and the root filesystem is read-only by default; `docker inspect <image>` shows no exposed ports other than the informational 8080.

description: `npx tsc --noEmit` exits 0; the project compiles under `strict: true` with `module: NodeNext` and `moduleResolution: NodeNext`. `package.json` declares `"type": "module"` and every internal import uses the explicit `.js` extension on the relative path (e.g. `from './transport/protocol.js'`).

description: A `relay-config.yaml` with `controlPlane.url = 'not-a-url'` and a valid `resources[0]` is loaded by `loadConfig()`; the Zod schema either accepts the URL string as-is (it is a free-form `z.string()`) or, when the Zod parse throws, the relay logs the parse error and exits non-zero — it never silently starts with an invalid control-plane URL. A YAML file with a missing `controlPlane.token` fails Zod validation with a clear error pointing at the missing field.

description: When no YAML file is present at `RELAY_CONFIG_PATH` and `/app/relay-config.yaml`, `loadConfig()` falls back to building the config from environment variables: it reads `CONTROL_PLANE_URL` (default `ws://localhost:3000/v1/relay/connect`), `RELAY_TOKEN`, `TENANT_ID`, walks `RESOURCE_0_*` / `RESOURCE_1_*` / ... until a `RESOURCE_N_ID` is missing, JSON-parses each `RESOURCE_N_CONNECTION`, and uses `RESOURCE_N_MAX_ROWS` (default 1000). The fallback config round-trips through the same Zod schema as the YAML path.

description: Environment variable interpolation: a YAML file containing `token: ${RELAY_TOKEN}` resolves to the value of `process.env.RELAY_TOKEN` at load time. Interpolation works at any nesting depth (string, object, array) and substitutes an empty string for unset variables (no exception is thrown for missing vars, matching the documented behavior).

description: `.github/workflows/ci.yml` is triggered on `pull_request` to `main`, runs `actions/setup-node@v4` with `node-version: '22'`, runs `npm ci`, validates PR commits with `npx commitlint --from $BASE_SHA --to $HEAD_SHA --verbose`, runs `npm run build`, and runs `docker build . --platform linux/amd64` as a smoke test. Each step exits 0 on a passing PR.

description: `.github/workflows/release.yml` is triggered on `push` to `main`. The `release` job runs `actions/checkout@v4` with `fetch-depth: 0`, sets up Node 22, runs `npm ci`, then `npx semantic-release` with `GITHUB_TOKEN`. If a new tag is produced, the workflow sets `released=true` and the version. The `docker` job (which `needs: release` and `if: needs.release.outputs.released == 'true'`) then sets up QEMU, sets up Docker Buildx, logs in to DockerHub with `DOCKERHUB_USERNAME` / `DOCKERHUB_TOKEN`, and runs `docker/build-push-action@v6` to push the image with tags `X.Y.Z`, `X.Y`, `X`, and `latest` for `linux/amd64,linux/arm64`.

description: `release.config.js` chains the documented semantic-release plugins in order: `@semantic-release/commit-analyzer` → `@semantic-release/release-notes-generator` → `@semantic-release/changelog` (writes CHANGELOG.md) → `@semantic-release/npm` (with `npmPublish: false`, only updates `package.json` version) → `@semantic-release/git` (commits `package.json` + `CHANGELOG.md` back with the `chore(release): ${nextRelease.version} [skip ci]` message) → `@semantic-release/github` (creates the GitHub Release). `branches: ['main']` restricts releases to the main branch.

description: The Dockerfile builds in two stages (`node:22-alpine AS builder` → `node:22-alpine`), installs devDependencies only in the builder stage, runs `npx tsc` to produce `dist/`, then copies `dist/` into the runtime stage, runs `npm install --omit=dev` to install only production dependencies, creates the `relay` user with `adduser -D -u 10001 relay`, sets `USER relay`, and starts with `CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]`. `WORKDIR /app` and `EXPOSE 8080` are declared.

description: When the WebSocket is open, `WsClient` sends a `heartbeat` message every 30 seconds with the same `relayId` (a UUID generated once in the `WsClient` constructor) and the configured `tenantId`. The heartbeat is constructed via `createHeartbeat(relayId, tenantId)` and matches the `HeartbeatMessage` shape `{ type: 'heartbeat', relayId, tenantId }`.

description: On WebSocket `open`, the relay sends a `resource_update` message containing every resource from the loaded config, each mapped to `{ resourceId, type: 'postgres' | 'mongodb', name, database, readOnly: true }`. The message is constructed via `createResourceUpdate(relayId, tenantId, resources)` and matches the `ResourceUpdateMessage` shape.

description: On any non-intentional WebSocket `close`, the `WsClient` schedules a reconnect via `setTimeout` with the current `reconnectDelay` (starting at 1000ms, doubling up to `maxReconnectDelay = 30000`), and the next `connect()` is invoked from the timer. Calling `WsClient.close()` sets `intentionalClose = true` first, so an intentional close does not schedule a reconnect.

description: The `connect()` method appends `?token=<token>&tenantId=<tenantId>` to the parsed URL and sets the headers `Authorization: Bearer <token>` and `X-Tenant-Id: <tenantId>` on the `new WebSocket(url, { headers })` call. A log line at info level (`'Connected to control plane'`) is emitted on the `open` event with `relayId` and `url` attached; on `error`, an error-level log is emitted; on `close`, an info log plus a `scheduleReconnect` is invoked unless `intentionalClose` is set.

description: On a `message` event, the `WsClient` JSON-parses the payload (string or buffer). Messages that fail to parse are logged at warn with the parse error and dropped. Messages where `parsed.jsonrpc !== '2.0'` or `parsed.method` is missing are likewise dropped. Only well-formed JSON-RPC 2.0 requests are forwarded to `opts.onMessage`.

description: A JSON-RPC 2.0 `list_resources` request (no params) returns a list of resources shaped as `{ resourceId, type, name, database, readOnly: true }[]` derived from the policy engine's resource list. description: A JSON-RPC 2.0 `describe_resource` request with `{ resourceId: '<unknown>' }` returns a JSON-RPC error with code `-32602` and message `Unknown resource: <id>` (via `createErrorResponse`). A valid `resourceId` triggers `driver.execute({ operation: 'list_tables', params: {} })` on the matching driver and returns `{ tables, type, database }`.

description: A JSON-RPC 2.0 `health_check` request returns an array of `{ resourceId, type, healthy, latencyMs }[]` produced by `HealthReporter.checkAll()`, one entry per initialized driver. If a driver's `healthCheck()` throws, that entry is logged at warn with `{ err, resourceId }` and included with `healthy: false` and the measured latency; the other drivers' results are unaffected.

description: A JSON-RPC 2.0 request with an unknown method (anything not in `['execute', 'list_resources', 'describe_resource', 'health_check']`) returns a JSON-RPC error with code `-32601` (method not found) and the message `Unknown method: <method>`. A request that throws inside the handler is caught, logged at error with `{ err, requestId }`, and answered with code `-32603` and the error's `message` (or `'Internal error'` for non-Error throws).

description: A JSON-RPC 2.0 `execute` request with `params.resourceId = 'main-pg'`, `params.operation = 'query'`, and `params.params = { sql: 'SELECT id, status FROM orders' }` returns a JSON-RPC 2.0 response whose `result` shape is `{ rows, rowCount, fields, executionTimeMs, masked: <maskedFieldCount > 0>, maskedFieldCount }`. The `result.masked` and `result.maskedFieldCount` reflect the masking step's output.

description: With a Postgres resource configured, an `execute` request with `operation: 'list_tables'` runs `SELECT table_name, table_type FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = 'public' ORDER BY table_name` against the configured database and returns the rows as `rows`, with `fields: [{ name: 'table_name', type: 'text' }, { name: 'table_type', type: 'text' }]`.

description: With a Postgres resource configured, an `execute` request with `operation: 'describe_table'` and `params.params.tableName = 'orders'` runs the information_schema.columns + information_schema.table_constraints queries scoped to the `public` schema and the named table, merging the column rows and the constraint rows (with each constraint row tagged `_type: 'constraint'`) into the response `rows`.

description: With a Postgres resource configured, an `execute` request with `operation: 'query'` and a single SELECT statement runs inside `BEGIN READ ONLY; SET statement_timeout = '30s'; <sql> LIMIT <n>; COMMIT;` on a dedicated `client` from the pool, and the response carries the rows, `rowCount`, `fields` (each `{ name, type }` derived from `pg`'s `dataTypeID`), and the measured `executionTimeMs`. The client is released back to the pool in a `finally` block; any error triggers `ROLLBACK` (best-effort) before the throw.

description: With a Postgres resource configured, an `execute` request with `operation: 'query'` and `params.params.limit = N` clamps the executed limit to `min(N, resource.maxRowsPerQuery)` and appends `LIMIT <clamped>` to the SQL. The clamped limit is enforced both at the policy engine (rejection when `N > maxRowsPerQuery`) and at the driver (defensive clamp in the SQL).

description: With a Postgres resource configured, an `execute` request with `operation: 'explain'` runs `EXPLAIN ANALYZE <sql>` and returns the explain rows verbatim. (The pg-query-parser also runs on `explain`; multi-statement and non-SELECT-shaped queries are rejected the same way as for `query`.)

description: `validateQuery("SELECT 1")` returns `{ valid: true }`. `validateQuery("INSERT INTO t VALUES (1)")` returns `{ valid: false, reason: 'Only SELECT statements are allowed, got INSERT' }` (or a textual `'INSERT statements are not allowed'` fallback if the parser throws). `validateQuery("SELECT 1; DROP TABLE users")` returns `{ valid: false, reason: 'Multi-statement queries are not allowed' }`.

description: `validateQuery("SELECT pg_sleep(10)")`, `validateQuery("SELECT * FROM pg_proc WHERE proname = 'pg_read_file'")` (containing the substring `pg_read_file`), and any query whose lowercased text contains one of the documented dangerous function names (`pg_sleep`, `lo_import`, `lo_export`, `pg_read_file`, `pg_write_file`, `pg_read_binary_file`, `pg_ls_dir`, `pg_stat_file`, `pg_terminate_backend`, `pg_cancel_backend`, `pg_reload_conf`, `set_config`, `current_setting`, `dblink`, `dblink_exec`) returns `{ valid: false, reason: 'Dangerous function detected: <name>' }`.

description: `validateQuery("WITH x AS (SELECT 1) SELECT * FROM x")` returns `{ valid: true }`. `validateQuery("SELECT * FROM (SELECT pg_sleep(1)) s")` is rejected via the dangerous-function text scan. `validateQuery("EXPLAIN SELECT 1")` is accepted at the parser level (its operation is `query` or `explain` at the driver; the parser only checks for SELECT/CTE shape).

description: With a MongoDB resource configured, an `execute` request with `operation: 'describe_table'` and `params.params.tableName = 'orders'` infers a schema from up to 10 sample documents via `collection.find().limit(10).toArray()`, walks the documents to record the set of `typeof value` per field, and merges those rows with the `indexes()` output (each tagged `_type: 'index'`, `{ name, key }`).

description: With a MongoDB resource configured, an `execute` request with `operation: 'query'` accepts either `params.params.filter` (used directly as the find filter) or `params.params.sql` (JSON.parsed into a filter when `filter` is empty), falls back to `{}` if neither is supplied, and runs `collection.find(query).limit(<clamped limit>).toArray()`. The result `rows` is the array of found documents cast to `Record<string, unknown>[]`.

The same applies to `$merge`. A pipeline of only `$match` / `$group` / `$sort` / `$project` / `$limit` stages is accepted.

description: An `execute` request with `params.params.limit = 5000` against a resource with `maxRowsPerQuery: 1000` is rejected by the policy engine with `reason: 'Row limit 5000 exceeds maximum 1000'`. An `execute` request with `params.params.limit = 100` (or no limit, which falls back to `maxRowsPerQuery`) is accepted; the driver then clamps the actual `LIMIT` (or Mongo `find().limit`) to `min(100, 1000) = 100`.

description: `MaskingEngine.mask(['john@example.com', '123.456.789-00', 'plain'])` returns `{ masked: ['***@***.***', '***.***.***-**', 'plain'], maskedFieldCount: 2 }` when masking is enabled with the default patterns. The masked output round-trips: the email regex strips the local part + domain, the CPF regex matches the dotted pattern, and the plain string is left alone.

description: `MaskingEngine.mask({ user: { email: 'a@b.co', notes: 'hello' } })` recursively walks the object, masks the email field, leaves `notes` alone, and returns `{ masked: { user: { email: '***@***.***', notes: 'hello' } }, maskedFieldCount: 1 }`. Arrays nested inside objects are also walked.

description: Every successful `execute` request writes a pino info entry with `{ timestamp, relayId, requestId, resource, operation, result: 'success', rowCount, maskedFieldCount, executionTimeMs }`. The `relayId` is the same UUID the WS client generated at startup and is reused across the lifetime of the process. The audit logger is created inside the `onMessage` callback for every request, so it carries the same `relayId` and a per-request `requestId`.

description: Every denied request (policy engine denial, driver validation failure, unknown resource) writes a pino warn entry with `result: 'denied'` and `policyChecks.reason` set to the denial reason. Every errored request writes a pino error entry with `result: 'error'` (an unhandled error from the try/catch in `onMessage` also writes a top-level error log with `{ err, requestId }` before the response is sent).

description: `audit.level` accepts `'debug' | 'info' | 'warn' | 'error'` (default `'info'`). The pino logger is constructed with the chosen level and `base: { relayId }` so every entry is automatically tagged. Setting `audit.enabled = false` short-circuits `log()` and writes nothing.

description: Sending `SIGTERM` (or `SIGINT`) to a running relay triggers the shutdown handler: `WsClient.close()` is invoked first (which sets `intentionalClose = true`, clears the heartbeat interval and reconnect timer, and closes the WebSocket), then `driver.close()` is awaited for every initialized driver (errors are caught and ignored), then `process.exit(0)`. A second `SIGTERM` after the first completes has no effect (the process is already gone).
steps: Read the Acceptance Check AC-047 description in project_specs.xml to understand the contract

Confirm the output / state matches what AC-047 asserts; record the result for the harness journal

description: An unhandled error inside a request handler (for example, a driver throws a non-recoverable error during `execute`) is caught at the top of the `onMessage` callback, logged at error with `{ err, requestId }`, and the request is answered with a JSON-RPC error `{ code: -32603, message: <err.message || 'Internal error'> }`. The process stays running and the WS client is not closed.

description: A driver that fails to initialize (the `try`/`catch` around the driver construction in `main()`) is logged at error with `{ err, id: resource.id }` and skipped; the relay continues to start with the remaining successfully-initialized drivers and reports them in the next `resource_update` and `list_resources` response. A relay with zero successfully-initialized drivers still starts the WS client and answers `health_check` with an empty array (no resources to check).

description: Dev workflow: `npm install` then `npm run dev` (which runs `tsx watch src/index.ts`) starts the relay, watches `src/` for changes, and restarts the process on every save. Build workflow: `npm run build` produces a clean `dist/` tree mirroring `src/`. Run workflow: `npm start` runs `node dist/index.js` against the compiled output.

description: With a clean shell env (`env -i PATH=$PATH`), no `AWS_*`, `STRIPE_*`, `CLERK_*`, `LANGFUSE_*`, `SENTRY_*`, `SVIX_*`, `SLACK_*`, `COMPOSIO_*`, `MASTRA_*`, `SQS_*`, `DYNAMODB_*`, `STS_*`, or `KMS_*` env vars set, `docker compose up -d` from the relay repo root brings up exactly four services: `relay-control-plane-stub` (port 3000), `relay-postgres` (port 5432), `relay-mongo` (port 27017), and `relay` (the hardened image, no inbound port mapping). Within 60 seconds `docker compose ps` reports all four as `Up`, the relay's boot log contains the line `Connected to control plane`, and the stub's log contains `[stub] resource_update from relayId= resources= ` for `n >= 1`. Grepping the relay container's boot log (`docker compose logs relay`) for `amazonaws.com`, `stripe.com`, `clerk.`, `langfuse`, `sentry.io`, `svix.com`, `slack.com`, `composio`, `mastra`, or any other vendor hostname returns 0 matches; the only outbound URL the relay attempts to resolve is `ws://relay-control-plane-stub:3000/v1/relay/connect`.

Within 60 seconds `docker compose ps` reports all four as `Up`, the relay's boot log contains the line `Connected to control plane`, and the stub's log contains `[stub] resource_update from relayId= resources= ` for `n >= 1`. Grepping the relay container's boot log (`docker compose logs relay`) for `amazonaws.com`, `stripe.com`, `clerk.`, `langfuse`, `sentry.io`, `svix.com`, `slack.com`, `composio`, `mastra`, or any other vendor hostname returns 0 matches; the only outbound URL the relay attempts to resolve is `ws://relay-control-plane-stub:3000/v1/relay/connect`.

description: A new `docker-compose.yml` exists at the relay repo root and defines the four services with no `image: ministack`, no `image: localstack`, no `image: langfuse`, no AWS / Stripe / Clerk / Sentry / Langfuse / Svix / Slack / Composio / Mastra env vars anywhere. The `relay-control-plane-stub` is a small `node:22-alpine` + `ws` service built from `scripts/control-plane-stub/Dockerfile` (a one-line `COPY server.mjs .` + `CMD ["node", "server.mjs"]`), listens on `0.0.0.0:3000`, accepts the relay's WebSocket handshake on `/v1/relay/connect`, validates the `?token= &tenantId= ` query string against the same env vars the relay uses, and on a `resource_update` message from the relay logs `[stub] resource_update from relayId= resources= `. The stub's source (`scripts/control-plane-stub/server.mjs`) imports only `ws` and Node stdlib modules — no AWS SDK, no Stripe, no `@clerk/*`, no other vendor SDK.
steps: Verify AC-052: A new `docker-compose.yml` exists at the relay repo root and defines the four services with no `image: ministack`, no `image: localstack`, no `image: langfuse`, no AWS / Stripe / Clerk / Sentry / Langfuse / Svix / Slack / Composio / Mastra env vars anywhere. The `relay-control-plane-stub` is a small `node:22-alpine` + `ws` service built from `scripts/control-plane-stub/Dockerfile` (a one-line `COPY server.mjs .` + `CMD ["node", "server.mjs"]`), listens on `0.0.0.0:3000`, accepts the relay's WebSocket handshake on `/v1/relay/connect`, validates the `?token= &tenantId= ` query string against the same env vars the relay uses, and on a `resource_update` message from the relay logs `[stub] resource_update from relayId= resources= `. The stub's source (`scripts/control-plane-stub/server.mjs`) imports only `ws` and Node stdlib modules — no AWS SDK, no Stripe, no `@clerk/*`, no other vendor SDK.

description: After `docker compose up -d` from AC-051, the stub's startup script sends a JSON-RPC 2.0 `health_check` request to the relay over the open WebSocket. The relay answers with `{ jsonrpc: '2.0', id, result: [{ resourceId: 'order-pg', type: 'postgres', healthy: true, latencyMs: }, { resourceId: 'order-mongo', type: 'mongodb', healthy: true, latencyMs: }] }` (one entry per initialized driver). The stub prints the JSON to its log as `[stub] health_check result=...` and exits 0. A failure of either driver (e.g. the Postgres container is stopped) yields `healthy: false` for that entry while the other driver still reports `healthy: true`; the stub still exits 0 and prints the partial result.
steps: Verify AC-053: After `docker compose up -d` from AC-051, the stub's startup script sends a JSON-RPC 2.0 `health_check` request to the relay over the open WebSocket. The relay answers with `{ jsonrpc: '2.0', id, result: [{ resourceId: 'order-pg', type: 'postgres', healthy: true, latencyMs: }, { resourceId: 'order-mongo', type: 'mongodb', healthy: true, latencyMs: }] }` (one entry per initialized driver). The stub prints the JSON to its log as `[stub] health_check result=...` and exits 0. A failure of either driver (e.g. the Postgres container is stopped) yields `healthy: false` for that entry while the other driver still reports `healthy: true`; the stub still exits 0 and prints the partial result.

description: After `docker compose up -d` from AC-051, the stub sends a JSON-RPC 2.0 `execute` request to the relay with `params: { resourceId: 'order-pg', operation: 'query', params: { sql: 'SELECT 1 AS one' } }`. The relay executes the query against the local `relay-postgres` container (the `orders` table was created by `scripts/control-plane-stub/initdb/01-orders.sql` on first start) and returns `{ jsonrpc: '2.0', id, result: { rows: [{ one: 1 }], rowCount: 1, fields: [{ name: 'one', type: 'int4' }], executionTimeMs: , masked: false, maskedFieldCount: 0 } }`. The stub prints the result, then sends a second `execute` with `operation: 'list_tables'` and asserts the response `result.rows` contains an `orders` row. The stub script exits 0; both queries hit the local Postgres on the docker network, not an external service.
steps: Verify AC-054: After `docker compose up -d` from AC-051, the stub sends a JSON-RPC 2.0 `execute` request to the relay with `params: { resourceId: 'order-pg', operation: 'query', params: { sql: 'SELECT 1 AS one' } }`. The relay executes the query against the local `relay-postgres` container (the `orders` table was created by `scripts/control-plane-stub/initdb/01-orders.sql` on first start) and returns `{ jsonrpc: '2.0', id, result: { rows: [{ one: 1 }], rowCount: 1, fields: [{ name: 'one', type: 'int4' }], executionTimeMs: , masked: false, maskedFieldCount: 0 } }`. The stub prints the result, then sends a second `execute` with `operation: 'list_tables'` and asserts the response `result.rows` contains an `orders` row. The stub script exits 0; both queries hit the local Postgres on the docker network, not an external service.

description: A new `.env.example` is committed at the relay repo root and contains only the relay's own env vars: `RELAY_TOKEN` (default `harness-smoke-token`), `TENANT_ID` (default `harness-tenant`), `CONTROL_PLANE_URL` (default `ws://relay-control-plane-stub:3000/v1/relay/connect`), `PG_HOST` (default `relay-postgres`), `PG_PORT` (default `5432`), `PG_DATABASE` (default `relay`), `PG_USER` (default `relay`), `PG_PASSWORD` (default `relay`), `MONGO_URI` (default `mongodb://relay-mongo:27017`), `MONGO_DATABASE` (default `relay`), and the optional `MASKING_ENABLED` (default `true`) + `AUDIT_ENABLED` (default `true`) toggles. The file contains no `AWS_*`, `STRIPE_*`, `CLERK_*`, `LANGFUSE_*`, `SENTRY_*`, `SVIX_*`, `SLACK_*`, `COMPOSIO_*`, `MASTRA_*`, `SQS_*`, `DYNAMODB_*`, `STS_*`, or `KMS_*` entries. `grep -icE 'AWS_|STRIPE|CLERK|LANGFUSE|SENTRY|SVIX|SLACK|COMPOSIO|MASTRA|SQS|DYNAMODB|STS|KMS' .env.example` returns 0. `docker compose --env-file .env.example up -d` from a clean shell env brings all four services Up.

description: `scripts/control-plane-stub/server.mjs` is committed: it imports `ws` and Node stdlib only, opens a `WebSocketServer` on `0.0.0.0:3000`, on `connection` validates the `?token=&tenantId=` URL query against the expected env-var pair, on `message` JSON-parses the payload and routes by `type` (logging `resource_update` with `relayId` + `resources.length`, logging `heartbeat` with `relayId` at debug), and exposes a one-shot `npm run smoke` script that opens its own outbound WebSocket to the relay, sends `health_check` and the two `execute` round-trips from AC-053 / AC-054, asserts the responses, and exits 0 on success / non-zero on mismatch. The stub has zero runtime dependency on any vendor SDK and is built into a 50-line image by `scripts/control-plane-stub/Dockerfile`.

description: Regression: the 50 original acceptance checks (AC-001 through AC-050) still pass against the new open-source layout. Concretely: `npm install` then `npm run build` exits 0 and produces `dist/index.js`; `npx tsc --noEmit` exits 0; `docker build . --platform linux/amd64` succeeds and produces an image that starts as UID 10001 (per AC-002's invariants). The `relay-config.docker.yaml` referenced by `docker-compose.yml` is a thin wrapper around the existing `relay-config.example.yaml` (same controlPlane + resources shape, just with the docker-network hostnames); `loadConfig()` parses it without code changes. PII masking, the audit logger, the policy engine, the Postgres / Mongo drivers, the WebSocket transport, the JSON-RPC dispatch, and graceful shutdown all behave identically to the original 50-check contract — the open-source context adds the docker-compose plumbing on top, it does not modify the relay's runtime behavior.
steps: Verify AC-058: Regression: the 50 original acceptance checks (AC-001 through AC-050) still pass against the new open-source layout. Concretely: `npm install` then `npm run build` exits 0 and produces `dist/index.js`; `npx tsc --noEmit` exits 0; `docker build . --platform linux/amd64` succeeds and produces an image that starts as UID 10001 (per AC-002's invariants). The `relay-config.docker.yaml` referenced by `docker-compose.yml` is a thin wrapper around the existing `relay-config.example.yaml` (same controlPlane + resources shape, just with the docker-network hostnames); `loadConfig()` parses it without code changes. PII masking, the audit logger, the policy engine, the Postgres / Mongo drivers, the WebSocket transport, the JSON-RPC dispatch, and graceful shutdown all behave identically to the original 50-check contract — the open-source context adds the docker-compose plumbing on top, it does not modify the relay's runtime behavior.
